Carveyance · Industry FEATURE / 16 MIN READ

The Cost of
Being Interesting

Why does anyone buy an American car? And why, after a century of building some of the most desirable vehicles on earth, can't Detroit convert that desire into the one thing Toyota has? The answer is not that American cars are worse. It's that identity and reputation are built by opposite methods — and Detroit chose the one that doesn't compound.

By Carveyance Editorial · U.S. market analysis

Dependability, 2026
Buick, 1st
Highest-ranked mass-market brand, second year running
Five-year resale
Corvette, 3rd
In the entire U.S. market. Ahead of every Toyota
Brand loyalty
GM, 11 years
Consecutive wins for highest overall manufacturer loyalty
The Setup

Three Facts That
Ruin a Good Stereotype

Everybody knows the story. American cars are thirsty, unreliable and worthless the moment you drive them off the lot. Japanese cars run forever. European cars are exquisite and expensive. It is one of the most durable pieces of received wisdom in American consumer life, and most people have never checked it.

So here are three findings from the last eighteen months.

One. In J.D. Power's 2026 U.S. Vehicle Dependability Study — 33,268 owners of three-year-old vehicles — the highest-ranked mass-market brand in America was Buick, at 160 problems per 100 vehicles against an industry average of 204. It was the second consecutive year Buick took the segment. Chevrolet finished third at 178, and Cadillac was runner-up among premium brands. Two Detroit nameplates in the mass-market top three.

Two. In iSeeCars' 2026 analysis of roughly 950,000 five-year-old vehicles, the model that held its value third-best in the entire American market was the Chevrolet Corvette — behind only the Porsche 718 Cayman and the 911, and ahead of every single Toyota on the list.

Three. In the same 2026 dependability study, premium brands were less dependable than mass-market ones, and the gap widened to 17 problems per 100 vehicles. The expensive European badge on your neighbour's driveway now statistically breaks more often than a Chevrolet.

The stereotype, measured

Problems per 100 vehicles after three years — 2026 (lower is better)
J.D. Power 2026 U.S. Vehicle Dependability Study, released February 2026. Based on 33,268 original owners of 2023 model-year vehicles, surveyed December 2024–November 2025. Industry average 204 PP100; premium-segment average 217 PP100.

None of this means American cars are secretly the best in the world. It means the public's mental model is running about fifteen years behind the data — and that is the actual subject of this article.

Because there are really two questions here, and almost everybody conflates them.

The first is why anyone buys an American car in the first place — what the hook is, what emotional need it serves, why a 23-year-old with no memory of Route 66 still wants a Mustang.

The second is why that desire doesn't turn into a reputation — why Buick can win a dependability study and change nobody's mind, why the badge that inspires the most loyalty in the industry still can't shake the word "unreliable."

They look like two separate problems. They are the same problem, viewed from either end.
Part One

The Hook

What an American car actually sells — and why it has almost nothing to do with the spec sheet.

The Proposition

Not Transportation.
Identity.

Start with what the competition is selling, because the contrast is the whole point.

A European luxury car is positioned around discernment. Buy it and you are telling the world you understand engineering, that you have taste, that you occupy a certain rung. A Japanese car is positioned around judgement. Buy it and you are telling the world — and more importantly telling yourself — that you are not the sort of person who makes expensive mistakes.

An American car is positioned around neither. It sells capability: not what the vehicle is, but what it could do, on some day that may never arrive.

Look at what Detroit has put in its commercials for eighty years. Not cornering data. Not cabin acoustics. Not a comparison chart. Open highways, mountains, ranches, jobsites, boats, horse trailers, dirt roads, weather. The proposition is not "this is a well-made object." It is you can go anywhere — and the anywhere is as much psychological as geographic.

The capability you will never use

This is the mechanism, and it's worth stating plainly because it gets mocked when it should be understood.

American buyers ask a question other markets mostly don't: what could this vehicle do? Tow thirteen thousand pounds. Carry drywall. Cross Montana with four people and a dog. Pull a camper. Get through the flood, the blizzard, the washed-out road. Whether any of that happens in a given decade is not the point. The point is that the answer is yes.

It is easy to sneer at this — the truck that never leaves the suburb, the Wrangler that never leaves pavement. Don't. Optionality has real value, and people pay for it constantly in other parts of their lives: the insurance policy never claimed, the spare room rarely used, the savings account not touched. A vehicle that can handle the worst day you can imagine is a hedge against uncertainty, and hedges are a rational thing to buy.

What's distinctly American is that the hedge became the product.

Trucks are America's luxury car

Europe has Mercedes. Japan has Lexus. America has the full-size pickup, and this genuinely confuses people from anywhere else.

An $85,000 truck is not, for most of its buyers, a work vehicle. It is the domestic equivalent of an S-Class: the thing you buy when you have arrived. It signals competence, self-reliance, capacity, the ability to help someone move. And the interiors have followed the money — quilted leather, massaging seats, screens the size of a laptop, sound systems that would embarrass a luxury sedan.

The loyalty numbers confirm it isn't sentiment. In S&P Global Mobility's 30th annual loyalty analysis — 13.6 million new retail registrations — Ford's F-Series won both the light-duty and heavy-duty pickup categories, and four Ford models posted loyalty rates above 60%. The Chevrolet Equinox was the most loyal model in the entire industry at 43%. Industry-wide loyalty in the same period was 51%.

Detroit does not have a trust problem. Detroit has a trust distribution problem — and that distinction is the hinge of this whole article.

The names are verbs

Notice how the two philosophies name their products.

Europe uses coordinates: S-Class, 911, A6, X5. Codes for people who already know the system. America uses Bronco, Charger, Ram, Avalanche, Expedition, Explorer, Lightning, Raptor, Wrangler — animals, weather events, acts of exploration. They aren't model designations. They're descriptions of something happening.

The inheritance

Then there's the part that no marketing department can manufacture, and that Toyota, for all its virtues, has never quite replicated in America.

"We're a Ford family." "Nobody in our house drives a Chevy." My grandfather drove one. My dad had one. I learned to drive in one. Truck loyalty in particular runs down bloodlines, and it is the single most valuable asset Detroit owns — because it arrives free, renews itself, and cannot be bought by a competitor at any price.

It is fashionable to call this irrational. It isn't. Brand loyalty is a sensible response to a market with high switching costs and poor information — you are outsourcing a difficult judgement to people who know you and have already run the experiment. That's not sentiment overriding reason. That's reason using the only data it has.

And yes, some of it is the flag

Buying American is real as a motivation, and it is more textured than it looks from outside. Research on it consistently finds several overlapping drivers rather than simple nationalism: pride in domestic industry, attachment to specific towns and plants, a belief about where the paycheque ends up, and broader consumer preference for home-market goods.

For a great many buyers it is less about politics than about a supplier two counties over, or a brother-in-law on a line somewhere. Treating that as a punchline is both unkind and analytically lazy — supporting the employer of people you know is one of the older economic instincts there is.

Three propositions, side by side

To be precise about what follows: this is a description of how these brands have chosen to position themselves over decades of marketing. It is not a claim about national character, and it certainly isn't a claim about the people who buy them.

PositioningThe promise it makesWhat it asks the buyer to fear
German luxuryEngineering authority and social placement — you are among people who can tell the differenceBeing thought unsophisticated
Japanese mainstreamFreedom from second-guessing — this decision will not punish youRegret, and unplanned expense
AmericanCapability and independence — whatever tomorrow needs, you'll have itBeing unequipped when it matters
ItalianThat an ordinary journey deserves to be beautifulA life spent being sensible
SwedishConsidered design and the safety of the people in the carHarm coming to your family

All five are legitimate. All five have built enormous businesses. But only one of them has an obvious relationship to what the vehicle will be worth in six years — and it isn't the American one.

Part Two

The Bill

Why desire doesn't become reputation, and why reputation is the only thing that turns into money later.

The Mechanism

Resale Value Is a
System, Not a Message

Here is a reasonable theory, and one a lot of people hold: if American manufacturers marketed reliability and long-term ownership value as aggressively as they market horsepower and towing, buyers would come to trust them, and resale values would rise.

The theory is sound. It is also, on its own, insufficient — and understanding exactly why is the most useful thing in this article.

A residual value is not a description of a car. It is the market's collective forecast of how much someone else will want it later. That forecast is assembled from expected repair costs, how many used examples are hitting auctions, how heavily the new ones are discounted, how long the model survives before being replaced, dealer and parts support, fleet volume, fuel prices, regulation — and, underneath all of it, whether the buyer after that believes they'll be able to sell it on again.

Marketing moves exactly one variable in that stack: desirability. It cannot move the other nine.

Toyota's resale advantage was not produced by a campaign. It was produced by decades of the same cars behaving the same way, priced with relative discipline, in high enough demand that they didn't need to be dumped. Advertising amplified a signal that already existed. It did not create one.

Marketing is the amplifier. It was never the signal.

Reputation runs on inertia

Brand image is not what a manufacturer says. It's the accumulated residue of a few million small stories — my Camry went 220,000 miles; my uncle's transmission went at 80,000; my Cadillac lost half its value in three years; my Tacoma sold for nearly what I paid.

Each anecdote is a scrap of unofficial market research, and together they harden into a mental shortcut. Once that shortcut exists, new information gets filtered through it rather than replacing it. A study confirming what you already believe strengthens the belief. A study contradicting it gets filed as an exception.

Which is exactly what happens to Buick.

Buick is the whole argument in one brand

Buick has the results. What it doesn't have is the thing Toyota actually possesses, which is not a reputation for reliability but a repeatable, intergenerational proof story: old ones still visibly on the road, mechanics who recommend them, family members who vouch, resale listings that confirm it, and ranking after ranking telling people what they already believed.

Buick's results surprise people. Toyota's confirm them. That difference is worth more than any advertising budget.

And there's a second problem, which is that reliability attaches to brands but people buy models. Say "Toyota is reliable" and the mind immediately supplies Corolla, Camry, RAV4, Tacoma, 4Runner. Say "Buick is reliable" and most shoppers have nothing to attach it to.

That's not the public's fault. Detroit has repeatedly discontinued or repositioned the nameplates that would have carried the memory: Taurus, Fusion, Focus, Impala, Malibu, Cruze, Dart, 200, Regal, LaCrosse. Some deserved to die. But every retired nameplate also retires the accumulated consumer memory attached to it.

The Corolla is proof for the next Corolla. The 4Runner's reputation is priced into the next 4Runner before it reaches a showroom. That compounding is the entire asset, and Detroit has interrupted it over and over.

The scale of the retreat In 2025 the U.S. passenger-car fleet fell below 100 million vehicles for the first time since the 1970s, as buyers and manufacturers alike moved to light trucks. Detroit didn't just discontinue individual sedans — it largely exited the segment where reputation is cheapest to build and most durable once built.

The discount is part of the reputation

Suppose a vehicle carries a $50,000 sticker but routinely transacts with $6,000 on the hood. The market has now decided it is a $44,000 car. A one-year-old example cannot be valued against the fiction — it has to compete with a discounted new one. The apparent depreciation looks brutal, and the brand gets blamed for the mechanics of its own pricing.

Worse, the lesson is permanent. Once buyers learn to wait for the rebate, dealers need the rebate to move metal, used examples must be cheaper still, leasing companies mark residual assumptions down, owners see poor trade-in numbers, and those owners go tell everyone the brand depreciates.

This is not stupidity. It's a trap with real walls. Idling a plant is enormously expensive, so moving one more unit can be worth more this quarter than the brand equity it costs over ten years. That is where marketing strategy, manufacturing economics and resale value collide — and manufacturing usually wins.

Fleet volume, and the invisible flood

This is why two vehicles wearing the same badge can have completely opposite outcomes. A Corvette is scarce, emotionally wanted, and bought by people who look after it. A mainstream sedan may have been discounted hard, leased heavily and sold into rental fleets by the thousand. Identical bowtie. Entirely different economics.

Which is why "American cars depreciate" is not merely unfair — it's imprecise. The accurate version is stranger and more interesting: American brands produce some of the very best and some of the very worst residual performers in the market, because their product strategies are unusually fragmented.

The category is not the car

Average five-year depreciation by vehicle type, 2026
iSeeCars analysis of over 950,000 five-year-old vehicles sold March 2025–February 2026. Industry average 41.8%, an improvement of 3.8 points on 2025. MSRPs inflation-adjusted using BLS data.

Why European luxury falls fastest — and the twist

The conventional explanation is that luxury cars are at their peak the day they launch and become yesterday's news the moment the next one appears. That's part of it, but the sharper version is this: the first owner and the third owner are buying different products.

The new flagship buyer purchases current status, the newest technology, warranty cover, personalisation, dealer treatment, and the specific experience of having it now. The five-year-old buyer of the same car inherits an expensive, complex machine with none of the status, none of the warranty, ageing electronics, and a repair bill still calibrated to the original six-figure price. They are not buying the thing that was sold. So they won't pay for it.

Which is not to say European makers are relaxed about this. Residuals set lease payments, so a weak forecast directly raises the monthly cost of putting someone in a new car. Porsche in particular has spent decades protecting desirability through controlled supply and careful evolution — which is precisely why the 718 Cayman and 911 sit at the very top of the retention rankings.

Now the twist, and it is a genuinely recent development: in the 2026 dependability study, premium brands underperformed mass-market ones in seven of nine categories, with the premium average at 217 PP100 against a mass-market segment that Buick leads at 160. The complexity that justifies the price is now the thing generating the complaints.

The five reasons Detroit can't simply advertise its way out

Assume a manufacturer decided tomorrow to run hard on reliability. Five things would work against it.

One: the claim is a portfolio promise, and one model can break it. Buick can average well, but a buyer whose specific vehicle gives trouble does not care about an average. Because Detroit shares powertrains, electronics and platforms widely, a single bad transmission or infotainment stack can contaminate several nameplates at once.

Two: the data moves. A vehicle can look excellent at three years and expensive at eight. Dependability studies measure a snapshot — three-year-old cars, twelve months of reported problems. No manufacturer can responsibly build a permanent identity on one strong year. Toyota's claim survived because it kept being true across generations.

Three: buyers distrust self-congratulation. "Built Ford Tough" works because toughness is demonstrated through use and commercial credibility. "Buick is as reliable as Toyota" would sound defensive, and defensiveness reads as doubt. Brands change through proof, not contradiction.

Four: reliability can flatten the thing that made you desirable. Jeep buyers accept compromises for a specific experience. Corvette buyers prioritise occasion. Truck buyers trade fuel for capability. Marketing every brand primarily on dependability would sand off the emotional specialisation that is Detroit's actual advantage. The goal isn't to replace personality with reliability — it's to put reliability underneath the personality.

Five: promising it raises expectations faster than engineering can meet them. This is acute right now. If a manufacturer markets a vehicle as simultaneously revolutionary and bulletproof, every software defect becomes evidence of dishonesty rather than of a hard problem.

The part nobody wants to hear: reliability doesn't create desire

A car can be dependable and still depreciate, because resale requires that someone wants it. A mechanically excellent sedan with a dated design and no emotional pull will sit. A Wrangler with mediocre ratings holds value because it is recognisable, modifiable and effectively unsubstitutable.

Reliability answers will this become a burden? Desirability answers will anyone still want it? Strong residuals require both, and Toyota's best products — Tacoma, 4Runner, Tundra — happen to have both. So, on a different formula entirely, does the Corvette.

In fairness to the other side Toyota's approach has costs too, and it's worth naming them. The Tundra has spent two decades and enormous investment failing to meaningfully dent a full-size truck market Detroit still owns. Lexus has never generated the cultural charge of a German badge despite beating both on dependability for four straight years. And Toyota's caution — the same conservatism that built the reputation — left it visibly late to the electric transition. Consistency is a strategy with a bill of its own.
Part Three

The Loop

How depreciation quietly became Detroit's youth marketing budget — and what happens when it stops working.

The Synthesis

Identity Requires Reinvention.
Reputation Requires Boredom.

Put the two halves together and the real finding appears, and it isn't a criticism of anyone's engineering.

The thing that makes American cars desirable is structurally the thing that stops them building Toyota's kind of reputation.

Identity has to be renewed. It runs on the new Bronco, the resurrected Hummer, the record horsepower figure, the dramatic redesign, the announcement that everything has changed. That is not a flaw in Detroit's execution — it is the requirement of the product it sells. An identity brand that stops reinventing stops being interesting, and an American car that isn't interesting has no proposition left.

Reputation runs on the exact opposite. It compounds through sameness: the Corolla proving the case for the next Corolla, across four decades, with as little drama as possible. Its central event is a non-event — nothing happened, and the car kept working.

You cannot easily run both operating systems in one company. Detroit picked the one that sells cars this quarter, and it is very good at it. The bill arrives later, in the resale column, and it is paid by the owner rather than the manufacturer.

Nothing dramatic happened, and the car kept working. That is almost impossible to advertise, and enormously valuable to own.

Reputation runs on a fifteen-year clock

Compounding this: cars are not bought often enough for opinion to update quickly. The average vehicle on American roads is now 12.8 years old — 14.5 for passenger cars. Most people's impression of a manufacturer is formed by a vehicle built one or two product generations ago, and family memory runs longer still.

"My parents had one and it was always in the shop" can cost a sale two decades later. The factory can fix a problem in three years. The public may take fifteen to notice.

Why opinion updates so slowly

Average age of vehicles in operation, United States, 2025 (years)
S&P Global Mobility, May 2025. Vehicles in operation reached 289 million with a 4.5% scrappage rate. The U.S. passenger-car fleet fell below 100 million for the first time since the 1970s.

The buyer got older. Much older.

And now the shift that reframes everything above.

According to S&P Global Mobility, the 18–34 share of new-vehicle registrations has fallen from 12% in early 2021 to under 10%. Over the same period, buyers aged 55 and over climbed to nearly half of all new registrations — roughly 49%, up from about 45%, and holding the largest share for eight consecutive quarters.

Half the new-car market is now over 55. Fewer than one in ten new vehicles goes to anyone under 35.

Who actually buys new cars now

Share of U.S. new-vehicle registrations by buyer age
S&P Global Mobility, reported June 2025. The 18–34 group still registered roughly 1.1 million new vehicles in the twelve months to March. Drivers include record transaction prices, the average new-vehicle amount financed reaching $43,899 in Q1 2026, the return of student-loan payments, and the availability of alternatives.
<10%
of new-vehicle registrations now go to buyers aged 18–34
~49%
go to buyers aged 55 and over — the largest share for eight straight quarters
$43,899
record average amount financed on a new vehicle, Q1 2026

This has an obvious consequence that the industry rarely says out loud: American manufacturers are no longer designing new vehicles for young people. They are designing them for the only demographic that can clear a $60,000 transaction.

So where does the 23-year-old come from?

Which returns us to the question that started this. A 23-year-old buying a Mustang today has no memory of Route 66, no Steve McQueen, no post-war optimism, no first-hand connection to any of the imagery Detroit spent seventy years building.

They're buying one anyway. Just not a new one.

Used Mustangs. Used Chargers. Used Silverados, Camaros, Wranglers. The identity gets bought at the second or third owner, at a price the first owner subsidised. And here is the conclusion that only appears when you put both halves of this story together:

Depreciation is Detroit's youth marketing budget. The steepest cost on the balance sheet is quietly doing the brand's most important work.

Every complaint in Part Two — heavy discounting, fleet volume, weak residuals on emotional products — has an unintended second effect. It puts an American performance car within reach of a twenty-two-year-old who could never have signed for one new. That buyer inherits the identity without ever having been sold it, becomes an owner, joins the culture, and in fifteen years may well walk into a showroom.

Toyota, notably, does not have this pipeline in the same form. A used Corolla is a sensible first car. It is not an initiation.

The Corvette proves both arguments at once

The Corvette is scarce relative to demand, has no true competitor at its price, carries an unbroken identity across seventy years, is bought by people who maintain it, and has never been dumped into rental fleets. That is the Toyota formula reached by an entirely different road: not this will not punish you, but there is nowhere else to get this.

What actually holds value

Five-year depreciation, best and worst performers (2026)
iSeeCars, 2026 study of 950,000+ five-year-old vehicles. Toyota holds 10 of the 25 strongest retainers; only three luxury models make that top 25. Of the 25 fastest depreciators, 18 are luxury models and eight are EVs. Selected models shown; figures from the 2025 and 2026 editions.

What breaks the loop

Here's the risk, and it is not hypothetical.

The recruitment pipeline only works if the emotional products eventually become affordable. That requires them to exist at prices that depreciate into reach within five or six years — which is precisely what a $50,000 Mustang or a $45,000 Camaro used to do.

If the identity lineup narrows to six-figure trucks and expensive EVs, two things happen at once. The used pipeline thins out, because there are fewer cheap emotional cars entering it. And EV depreciation — currently averaging 57.2% over five years against an industry average of 41.8% — means the ones that do arrive are damaged goods carrying battery-health anxiety rather than desire.

Detroit has spent a century getting extraordinarily good at making people want things. The open question is whether it will keep making things that can still be wanted at eight years old and $14,000 — because that, not the showroom, is where the next generation of American car owners is actually made.

The Prescription

What Would Actually
Have to Change

None of this is a counsel of despair. It's a list of unglamorous, expensive, mostly boring commitments — which is exactly why they're hard, and exactly why they'd work.

Keep the nameplate alive

Refine core models incrementally instead of rebuilding the philosophy every cycle. Reputations compound through evolution and reset to zero on reinvention.

Protect the price

Constant rebates teach the market to devalue the product. Costly in the short term, because it means refusing easy volume — which is the whole reason it doesn't happen.

Aim fleet where it helps

Vans, work trucks and commercial vehicles gain credibility from visible hard use. Consumer models should not flood auctions.

Warranty as proof

A long warranty transfers risk from buyer to manufacturer and forces the maker to internalise poor quality. Hyundai and Kia used exactly this to rewrite their reputations.

Publish the real costs

Scheduled maintenance to 100,000 miles, parts pricing, warranty claim rates, repair times, actual residual performance. Make dependability something a buyer can check rather than something a brand asserts.

Support the second owner

Strong certified pre-owned programmes raise used demand, which raises auction values, which raises trade-ins and residual forecasts. The loop starts with the buyer you don't get paid by.

And the reason it mostly doesn't happen

Not incompetence. Incentives.

Public companies are judged on quarterly profit, annual share, factory utilisation, launch performance and investor expectation. Protecting residuals means refusing volume. Improving dependability can mean delaying technology. Keeping a nameplate means accepting a slow product cycle. Reducing complexity makes a new model look worse on the showroom spec sheet.

Every decision that builds a fifteen-year reputation is conservative, repetitive and uncomfortable inside a twelve-month reporting cycle. Toyota's real advantage was never that it advertised reliability more effectively. It was a corporate culture willing to treat consistency as innovation — and a governance structure that let it.

Detroit is drawn, culturally and financially, toward the comeback: the dramatic redesign, the new sub-brand, the revolutionary powertrain, the record number, the declaration that this time it's different. That instinct built the Corvette, the Mustang, the Bronco, the Raptor and the F-Series. It is not a defect. It is the source of everything valuable Detroit owns.

It just doesn't compound.

The Bottom Line

The Promise Detroit
Hasn't Made

So: could American manufacturers improve resale value by putting reliability, ownership cost and long-term support at the centre of their identity? Yes — but through a chain reaction, not a campaign, and on a timeline measured in product generations rather than quarters.

Better quality lowers expected repair risk. Longer warranties move the remaining risk off the buyer. Clearer evidence widens consideration. More buyers competing for used examples lifts auction prices. Higher auction prices improve trade-in offers. Better trade-ins make leases competitive. Better ownership economics sell more new cars. Owners become advocates. And somewhere around year ten, the reputation starts carrying itself.

Buick's results prove Detroit can already build the product. The public simply hasn't reorganised its beliefs around one ranking, and it never will, because that is not how beliefs work.

The missing message was never our cars are reliable now. Every manufacturer says that, and nobody has ever believed one for saying it. The missing message is harder, slower and considerably more expensive:

We will still stand behind this vehicle when it is no longer new, no longer fashionable, and no longer owned by the person we sold it to.

Until the product, the pricing, the dealer network, the warranty and the used-car strategy all make that sentence believable, no amount of advertising will land it.

And once they do, the advertisement won't be necessary.

Carveyance
Sources: J.D. Power 2026 U.S. Vehicle Dependability Study (33,268 respondents, 2023 model-year vehicles); iSeeCars 2025 and 2026 five-year depreciation studies (800,000+ and 950,000+ vehicles respectively); S&P Global Mobility 30th Annual Automotive Loyalty Awards (13.6 million new retail registrations, October 2024–September 2025); S&P Global Mobility vehicle-age and new-registration-by-age analyses (2025); Consumer Reports brand reliability rankings; Edmunds financing data. Market-share, depreciation and dependability figures vary by methodology and reporting period; where sources differed, ranges or the more conservative figure are used. Analysis and interpretation are the author's.
